narrative:
Ben Ray Lujan is the son of a former New Mexico state legislator who grew up in Nambe Pueblo country, north of Santa Fe, before becoming the first Latino to chair a national party campaign committee when Nancy Pelosi tapped him to run the DCCC in 2014 [1]. He rewarded her confidence by helping engineer the 2018 blue wave that flipped the House, recruiting a record-breaking class of freshmen [1]. Then he ran for the Senate, won, suffered a stroke five weeks into his first term, had a piece of his skull removed, and was back at work within a month — greeted with a standing ovation from colleagues on both sides of the aisle [2][3]. If nothing else, the man is difficult to keep down.

As Chair of the Subcommittee on Communications, Media, and Broadband, Lujan has made closing the digital divide his signature cause. He introduced the Digital Equity Foundation Act, the PLAN for Broadband Act, and legislation to ban predatory data caps that force rural families to pay premium prices for basic internet [4]. He has pushed internet service providers on user-privacy practices [5] and championed the Affordable Connectivity Program that brought $382 million in broadband funding to New Mexico [6]. It is a legislative portfolio perfectly tailored to a state where some communities still lack reliable cell service, let alone fiber.

On the 80% Bill's planks, Lujan is a standout on several fronts. He co-introduced the For the People Act and the Freedom to Vote Act with Heinrich, both of which ban partisan gerrymandering and include DISCLOSE Act provisions requiring dark-money donor transparency [7]. He cosponsored the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act [8]. He cosponsored the Social Security Fairness Act to repeal the WEP and GPO provisions that shortchanged public servants [9]. And in a move that should warm the heart of anyone who has ever been told their phone screen can only be fixed at an "authorized" store, Lujan introduced the Fair Repair Act in February 2026, requiring manufacturers to make diagnostic tools, parts, and manuals available to independent repair shops and consumers [10].

Where Lujan gets interesting is the tension between his broadband-crusader persona and the industries that fund Senate campaigns. As the senator overseeing telecommunications policy, his donor base inevitably includes the very companies whose pricing practices he criticizes [11]. His 2020 House cycle saw PAC money from a cross-section of industries that benefit from the committee assignments he holds [11]. He has not introduced comprehensive GDPR-style data-privacy legislation despite chairing the subcommittee most responsible for the topic, and he has not cosponsored a congressional term-limits amendment [1].

The stroke narrative is worth pausing on, not for sympathy but for context. Lujan's absence in early 2022 threatened to derail Supreme Court confirmation votes in a 50-50 Senate [2]. His rapid return demonstrated both personal grit and the fragility of a system where one senator's health emergency can paralyze an entire branch of government. That fragility is precisely the kind of structural problem the 80% Bill aims to address — though term limits, the plank most relevant here, remain conspicuously absent from Lujan's own legislative agenda.

Cynthia Lummis arrived in the Senate in 2021 as the first woman to represent Wyoming in the upper chamber, and she promptly devoted herself to the cause closest to her heart: making Bitcoin go up. Known as "the Bitcoin Senator," Lummis bought her first Bitcoin in 2013 on a tip from her son-in-law and has since become Congress's most enthusiastic cryptocurrency evangelist. She holds between $100,000 and $350,000 in Bitcoin personally, chairs the Senate Banking Subcommittee on Digital Assets, and has introduced legislation to create a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve that would have the federal government purchase one million Bitcoin over five years. It is the legislative equivalent of getting your grandma into crypto, except grandma controls a Senate subcommittee.

The conflict of interest is so obvious it barely needs stating, but let us state it anyway. Lummis personally profits from the same asset class she regulates. Her son-in-law Will Cole is deeply invested in Bitcoin and the adjacent industry. She disclosed a Bitcoin purchase late, blamed it on a "filing error," and later filed an amended financial disclosure after initially omitting her crypto holdings entirely. She now chairs the subcommittee that decides the regulatory future of the asset she owns. Ethics watchdogs have called it "definitely a conflict of interest," but in the Senate, conflicts of interest are less a disqualification than a job requirement.

On crypto regulation, Lummis has pushed the Lummis-Gillibrand bill to shift oversight from the SEC to the more industry-friendly CFTC, introduced provisions blocking the SEC from bringing lawsuits against existing tokens unless fraud is involved, and generally positioned herself as crypto's legislative bodyguard. Crypto VCs and executives from firms like Multicoin Capital and Andreessen Horowitz have rewarded her with generous campaign contributions. When FTX collapsed, Lummis argued her bill would have prevented it — a claim that conveniently requires passing her bill to test.

Beyond crypto, Lummis is a reliable vote against every plank of the 80% Bill. She voted NO on paid parental leave for federal employees. She opposes environmental regulation, preferring "free market innovation" over government action on climate — a stance that aligns neatly with Wyoming's coal and oil interests. She voted to acquit Trump during his second impeachment and against the American Rescue Plan. Her Heritage Action score and AFL-CIO ratings confirm she votes with corporate interests against working families with impressive consistency.

Lummis announced in December 2025 that she will not seek reelection in 2026, giving her a final sprint to enshrine crypto-friendly regulations before leaving office. Wyoming will lose its Bitcoin Senator, but the industry will keep the laws she wrote for them.

James Lankford is Oklahoma's senior senator, a former Baptist youth camp director who traded saving souls for saving the Republican conference from itself — a task that has proven considerably more difficult. He chairs the Senate Ethics Committee, which is a bit like being the designated driver at a party where everyone insists they are sober. He also sits on the Finance Committee, the Intelligence Committee, and the Homeland Security Committee, giving him jurisdiction over taxes, spying, and borders, which is roughly the trifecta of issues most likely to get a Republican censured by his own state party [1].

And censured he was. In January 2024, the Oklahoma GOP voted to formally condemn Lankford for the unforgivable sin of negotiating a bipartisan border security bill with Democrats. The bill would have tightened asylum standards and added emergency border authority, but Donald Trump publicly opposed it because he did not want President Biden to claim a legislative win before the election. Senate Republicans obediently killed their own bill, and Lankford — the man who spent months writing it — was left holding the legislative equivalent of a participation trophy [2]. The state party later admitted the censure vote may not have followed proper procedure, which is the Oklahoma GOP's way of saying "we meant it, but technically we did it wrong" [3].

On the 80% Bill, Lankford is a study in selective populism. He voted for the Social Security Fairness Act and has spoken about the need to shore up the program's solvency — a position that puts him slightly ahead of colleagues who prefer to pretend the trust fund will fix itself [4]. As Ethics Committee chairman, he has clashed with Senator Josh Hawley over enforcement of stock trading disclosure rules, though the committee's actual enforcement record remains about as aggressive as a sternly worded Post-it note [5].

Where Lankford falls short is everywhere else. He has opposed the DISCLOSE Act and campaign finance transparency measures. He has taken corporate PAC money throughout his career, with oil and gas among his top donor industries [6]. He has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, supporting paid family leave, auditing the Pentagon, banning junk fees, or enacting term limits. On the Epstein files, he has offered vague calls for transparency without pushing for full release. Lankford is the kind of senator who will occasionally do the right thing — and then watch quietly as his party buries it in a shallow legislative grave.

Mike Lee is the rare senator who has managed to be on the right side of exactly two issues — auditing the Pentagon and term limits — while being catastrophically wrong on nearly everything else the 80% Bill stands for. In 2021, Lee co-introduced the Audit the Pentagon Act alongside Bernie Sanders and Chuck Grassley, declaring that "the Pentagon should not be permitted to access an ever-increasing sum of money every year without meeting the same financial accounting standards as every other federal department." A fine statement. Truly inspiring. Now consider that Lee has spent the rest of his career working to dismantle the social safety net that keeps millions of Americans alive.

In 2010, Lee told voters he wanted to "phase out Social Security, to pull it up from the roots and get rid of it," adding that "Medicare and Medicaid are of the same sort, they need to be pulled up." When President Biden called him out on this during the 2023 State of the Union, Lee waved his hands and claimed he was taken out of context. The video, of course, speaks for itself. Lee has proposed raising the Medicare eligibility age from 65 to 68, suggested privatizing Social Security by letting people divert payments into private accounts, and released budget plans that the Center on Budget and Policy Priorities said would "sharply cut Social Security, Medicare, and other programs while making the tax system less progressive."

On term limits, Lee's hypocrisy is a masterclass. He campaigned in 2010 on limiting senators to two terms, co-sponsored term limit legislation multiple times, then conveniently stopped co-signing the latest iteration as his own third term approached. The Salt Lake Tribune published an op-ed titled "Sen. Mike Lee's hypocrisy on term limits" that laid it out plainly: the man loves term limits right up until they apply to him.

Lee called the For the People Act — which would have addressed dark money, voting rights, and campaign finance reform — "as if written in Hell by the devil himself." Meanwhile, his own campaigns have benefited from dark money organizations, including a 501(c)(4) group called Liberty Champions that funneled undisclosed donations his way. He opposes the DISCLOSE Act, opposes banning corporate PACs, and has received substantial contributions from lobbyists throughout his career.

On public lands, Lee wants to sell off federal territory, arguing the government "owns too much land" — a position that plays well with extraction industries but horrifies the hikers, hunters, and conservationists who actually use that land. Utah's senior senator talks like a libertarian populist but governs like a corporate one.

Ted Lieu has represented the westside of Los Angeles -- from Malibu to Manhattan Beach, Beverly Hills to Torrance -- since 2015, and has built a national profile as one of the Democratic Party's most prolific tweeters and sharpest Trump-era antagonists. A retired Air Force colonel who spent 25 years in the JAG Corps, Lieu serves as House Democratic Caucus Vice Chair, a party leadership post, and sits on the Judiciary Committee and the Foreign Affairs Committee [1][2]. He is an immigrant from Taiwan who arrived in the U.S. at age three, graduated from Stanford and Georgetown Law, and has never been shy about reminding people of all of it. In the 119th Congress, he has missed only 3 of 449 roll call votes and broken with the party exactly once [3].

On the 80% Bill, Lieu's alignment is broader than most. He has called explicitly for repealing the 2001 AUMF, tweeting that no president should be able to "drag us into conflict without proper authorization" -- a position rooted in his military service and his belief that "Congress needs to reassert our war powers authority" [4][5]. He introduced the Restricting First Use of Nuclear Weapons Act to prevent the president from launching a first nuclear strike without congressional approval [6]. He co-sponsored the DISCLOSE Act to unmask dark money in elections, and joined a lawsuit challenging the SpeechNow ruling that created the super PAC loophole -- making him one of a handful of sitting members to literally take Citizens United to court [7][8]. He introduced the EMPOWER Act to modernize presidential public financing with a 6:1 small-dollar match, directly targeting the outsized influence of wealthy donors [9]. He supports Medicare drug price negotiation, praising the Inflation Reduction Act's provisions, and serves on the Affordable Prescription Drug Task Force [10]. He has vocally championed Social Security protection as a co-sponsor of the Social Security 2100 Act [11]. And on the Epstein files, Lieu has been perhaps the most aggressive voice in Congress, publicly citing allegations in the files, calling for AG Pam Bondi to release them, and demanding a special counsel investigation after accusing Bondi of lying under oath about the contents [12][13].

Where Lieu gets quieter is on the structural reforms that would constrain his own class. He has not championed a congressional stock trading ban, a lifetime lobbying ban, or term limits -- the kinds of reforms that would clip the wings of a party leader building a long career in the House. He has not made Pentagon audit reform a priority, despite sitting on committees with adjacent jurisdiction. His voting record is nearly flawless by party-line standards, but party loyalty and populist reform are not the same thing, and the 80% Bill includes several items -- taxing the ultra-wealthy, closing the carried interest loophole, banning corporate purchases of single-family homes -- where Lieu has simply not shown up with legislation or loud advocacy.

The donor picture explains some of the silence. Lieu's top contributing industries include lawyers and law firms, and he has acknowledged the entertainment industry's support, once thanking the MPAA directly for backing his campaigns [14]. He co-authored California's film tax credit legislation and has been a "fierce advocate" for Hollywood production incentives -- a reasonable position for a Los Angeles congressman, but one that aligns neatly with the preferences of his donor base [15]. His Victory Fund's top 2024 contributor was the law firm Russ, August & Kabat at $30,100 [16]. He is not awash in corporate PAC money the way some members are, but his leadership position means he is deeply embedded in the party fundraising apparatus, distributing PAC-to-PAC contributions to fellow Democrats and benefiting from the same dark money ecosystem he claims to oppose.

Lieu is the rare member who has put real skin in the game on campaign finance -- you do not join a lawsuit against Citizens United as a political stunt -- and his work on the Epstein files and war powers has genuine teeth. But his silence on stock trading bans, term limits, and the carried interest loophole suggests a familiar calculus: fight hard on the reforms that burnish your brand, go quiet on the ones that threaten your career trajectory. The 80% Bill is a test of whether members will back the full menu of what Americans want, not just the specials. Lieu orders impressively, but he skips a few courses.

Barry Loudermilk has represented Georgia's 11th Congressional District since 2015, bringing to Washington the resume of an Air Force veteran, IT company founder, and former Georgia state legislator who served in both the state House and Senate before making the jump to Congress [1]. He sits on the House Financial Services Committee, where he is Vice Chair of the Subcommittee on Financial Institutions, and on the Committee on House Administration, where he chairs the Oversight Subcommittee. In February 2026, Loudermilk announced he would not seek reelection, which means his final term is unencumbered by the need to please voters -- a freedom that, in theory, could liberate a congressman to do the right thing, and in practice has not noticeably done so [2].

On the twenty provisions of the 80% Bill, Loudermilk's record is a near-perfect inversion of what his constituents would likely support if asked. His most memorable contribution to the congressional stock trading debate came during an April 2022 hearing, when he argued against banning the practice by comparing it to murder laws: "If we enact a law that prohibits members from Congress from owning or trading stocks, it will still happen by bad players," he reasoned, apparently unaware that this is an argument against all legislation [3]. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation supported by roughly 80 percent of Americans across party lines [4]. He has not cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act, has shown no interest in banning corporate PACs or dark money, and has never championed a lifetime lobbying ban for former members. On January 6, Loudermilk voted to reject the electoral votes from Arizona and Pennsylvania in the hours after the Capitol was stormed, then later used his chairmanship of the House Administration Oversight Subcommittee to investigate the investigators -- releasing reports focused less on the insurrection itself than on clearing his own name regarding a Capitol tour he gave to constituents the day before the attack [5].

Where Loudermilk does align with popular sentiment, it is largely rhetorical. He speaks the language of government accountability and fiscal responsibility, yet he has not pushed for a genuine Pentagon audit despite the Department of Defense failing every annual audit since they began in 2018 [6]. He talks about protecting Americans from government overreach while sponsoring the New BANK Act and the Financial Reporting Threshold Modernization Act -- legislation designed to loosen Bank Secrecy Act reporting requirements and restrain the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, which is to say, to make life easier for the banks that fund his campaigns rather than the consumers those agencies were created to protect [7].

Follow the money and the picture sharpens considerably. Loudermilk's top contributing industry in the 2023-2024 cycle was commercial banks, which gave him $81,550, followed by securities and investment firms, real estate, and insurance [8]. The American Bankers Association, Mortgage Bankers Association, Truist, and PNC are among his reliable PAC backers, alongside corporate giants like Home Depot, Delta, and Coca-Cola. AIPAC contributed $43,100. His leadership PAC draws nearly two-thirds of its funds from PAC contributions, with small-dollar donations under $200 accounting for a vanishing 1.91 percent [9]. For a man who sits on the committee that regulates the financial industry, the arrangement is less a conflict of interest than a business model.

The bottom line on Barry Loudermilk is that he is a congressman who has spent a decade on the Financial Services Committee doing exactly what the financial services industry would want him to do, while opposing virtually every measure in the 80% Bill that might disrupt the cozy relationship between Congress and its corporate benefactors. He argued that banning stock trades is pointless because bad actors will cheat anyway, voted to overturn a presidential election, and used his oversight gavel to investigate the people who investigated an insurrection. He is leaving Congress in 2026, but the system he helped protect is not going anywhere.

Darin LaHood represents Illinois's 16th Congressional District, a sprawling, largely rural swath of central and northwestern Illinois that includes Peoria, Bloomington, and Springfield. He won a 2015 special election to replace Aaron Schock, who resigned amid spending scandals, and has held the seat comfortably since [1]. The son of former Transportation Secretary Ray LaHood, he serves on the powerful House Ways and Means Committee, where tax policy is written — a detail that becomes relevant when you examine which of the 80% Bill's twenty proposals he has chosen to champion and which he has chosen to ignore [2].

LaHood's record on the 80% Bill is a masterclass in selective populism. He has supported congressional term limits, cosponsoring a constitutional amendment to cap House members at three terms and senators at two [3]. He backed the release of the Epstein files and voted for the bipartisan resolution demanding transparency [4]. He has supported protections for U.S. farmland from foreign adversaries, a natural fit for a district where agriculture is the economic backbone [5]. He voted in favor of police body camera funding through the Invest to Protect Act [6]. These are the easy wins — positions that play well in a conservative rural district without threatening a single donor relationship.

The harder questions are where LaHood goes silent. From his perch on Ways and Means, he has actively opposed efforts to close the carried interest loophole, which allows hedge fund managers to pay lower tax rates than teachers [7]. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions [8]. He has not cosponsored any bill to ban congressional stock trading, despite the issue polling above 85% with voters of both parties [9]. On banning corporate PACs, ending dark money, and the DISCLOSE Act, his record is a clean sheet of inaction [10]. The AUMF repeal to end forever wars has not attracted his support, nor has any meaningful version of paid family leave [11]. Taxing the ultra-wealthy is, from LaHood's committee chair, essentially a non-starter — he has voted against every major progressive tax proposal that has reached the floor [7].

The financial picture is clarifying. LaHood's top donors read like a directory of industries that benefit from the status quo he protects. For the 2024 cycle, his top contributing sectors included insurance, securities and investment, and real estate [12]. Corporate PACs from Caterpillar, State Farm, and the American Bankers Association have been consistent supporters [13]. He raised over $2.8 million, with PAC money representing a substantial share of his total haul [12]. When a member of the committee that writes the tax code receives his largest checks from the financial sector, his opposition to closing tax loopholes stops looking like principle and starts looking like product placement.

LaHood is a useful case study in how a representative can endorse the most popular items on the 80% Bill — term limits, Epstein files, farmland protection — while systematically blocking the structural reforms that would actually redistribute power away from the donor class. He will term-limit himself in theory while ensuring that the carried interest loophole, corporate PAC money, and dark money spending remain untouched for whoever holds the seat next [3][7][10].

Susie Lee would like you to know she "strongly supports a ban on Members of Congress directing trades of individual stocks" [1]. What she'd prefer you not dwell on is the roughly 200 stock transactions totaling over $3.3 million she failed to properly disclose while serving in Congress, violating the STOCK Act more times than most people check their brokerage app [2]. Her spokesperson assured the public these trades were "executed by a third party money manager with no input whatsoever from the congresswoman" [3] -- a defense that might carry more weight if her then-husband, Dan Lee, hadn't been the CEO of Full House Resorts, a casino company whose stock she held millions of dollars worth of [4].

The casino connection gets richer. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Lee joined Nevada's congressional delegation in lobbying the Small Business Administration to let small gaming businesses access Paycheck Protection Program loans. Two weeks later, Full House Resorts -- her husband's company -- collected $5.6 million in PPP funds [5]. The stock, which had cratered from $3.59 to $0.53, subsequently rebounded. An ethics complaint followed. Lee's counter-move was announcing she was "ending her marriage" -- though the divorce wasn't actually finalized for another three years, conveniently wrapping up just weeks before the 2024 election [6].

On the 80% Bill's core issues, Lee's record is the portrait of a lawmaker who talks reform while cashing the checks. She co-sponsored prescription drug pricing legislation in 2024 [7] and has voted with Democrats on the Inflation Reduction Act's drug negotiation provisions. But on the stock-trading ban that would most directly affect her own portfolio? She has expressed verbal support but has not cosponsored the leading House bill to ban congressional trading [8]. Her top industry contributors include real estate, securities, and the finance sector -- the same industries that benefit most when members of Congress can trade on inside information [9].

Lee voted for the Freedom to Vote Act and the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act [10], and she voted for the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act [11]. On paper, that's a solid reform record. In practice, she's a reminder that the 80% Bill exists precisely because popular positions and personal conduct are often two very different things in Congress.

Mike Levin represents California's 49th Congressional District, a sun-soaked coastal stretch running from south Orange County through Camp Pendleton to northern San Diego County. An environmental attorney by trade -- Stanford undergrad, Duke Law, years spent on clean energy policy -- Levin flipped the seat in 2018 after longtime Republican Darrell Issa retired, and has held it since [1][2]. He sits on the Natural Resources Committee and the Veterans' Affairs Committee, belongs to both the Congressional Progressive Caucus and the New Democrat Coalition, and has been rated among the top five percent most effective lawmakers in the House by the nonpartisan Center for Effective Lawmaking [3]. He is also a proud member of the End Corruption Caucus, a group dedicated to getting dark money and corporate PACs out of politics [4]. On paper, Levin is exactly what the 80% Bill is looking for.

And to his credit, the paper holds up better than most. Levin has been one of Congress's loudest voices demanding a ban on congressional stock trading, delivering floor speeches on the subject and calling the practice "fundamentally wrong" [5][6]. He and his wife sold all their individual stocks before he took office and he has maintained a commitment not to trade while serving [7]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation and a 15% corporate minimum tax -- checking off multiple 80% Bill priorities in a single vote [8]. He has cosponsored the Social Security 2100 Act and the Social Security Fairness Act to protect and expand benefits [9][10]. He backed the For the People Act, which addresses gerrymandering, dark money, and election integrity [4]. He has publicly demanded the full release of the Epstein files, calling the DOJ's withholding of over two million documents "a cover-up" [11]. On the carried interest loophole, he supported closing it through the IRA's tax provisions. That is a substantial alignment with the 80% Bill's agenda -- more than most of his colleagues can claim.

Levin took the No Corporate PAC pledge, and unlike many who take it and then find creative workarounds, his fundraising reflects it: the majority of his money comes from individual donors rather than PAC contributions [12][13]. That said, the pledge is not airtight. In 2021, Roll Call reported that roughly $6 million had flowed to No Corporate PAC pledge-takers through loopholes, including leadership PACs and party committees funded in part by corporate money, and Levin was among the Democrats named [14]. His PAC contributions come largely from unions, ideological groups, and other lawmakers' PACs -- cleaner than the corporate pipeline, but not quite the small-dollar paradise his campaign literature might suggest.

The bottom line on Levin is that he is one of the closer fits to the 80% Bill in the California delegation. He has put votes behind the stock trading ban, Medicare negotiation, Social Security expansion, campaign finance reform, and Epstein transparency. He walks the walk on refusing corporate PAC money, even if the walk occasionally detours through a loophole or two. The 80% Bill asks whether representatives will champion what supermajorities of Americans want. Levin's answer is mostly yes -- and in Congress, "mostly yes" with receipts is rarer than it should be.

Julia Letlow is the Republican representative for Louisiana's 5th Congressional District, a seat she won in a 2021 special election after her husband Luke died of COVID-19 complications just days before he would have been sworn in. It is a genuinely tragic origin story, and Letlow has leaned into it with a brand built around faith, family, and her background as a university administrator. She sits on the House Appropriations Committee and has earned a reputation as a relatively pragmatic member of the GOP conference, supporting the Heritage Foundation's agenda about 87% of the time in her first term -- just below the Republican average [1].

On the 80% bills, Letlow deserves real credit on two fronts. She is a member of the bipartisan Paid Family Leave Working Group, one of just six House members -- three from each party -- exploring legislative frameworks to guarantee paid leave for American workers [2]. She also championed the Social Security Fairness Act, which eliminated the Windfall Elimination Provision and Government Pension Offset that slashed benefits for teachers, police officers, and other public servants [3]. Those are genuine populist wins. But on Medicare drug negotiation, the DISCLOSE Act, banning corporate PACs, taxing the ultra-wealthy, and virtually every other item on the 80% list, Letlow has either voted with party leadership or stayed silent. She opposed the Inflation Reduction Act, calling it "more taxes and a weaponized IRS" [4], effectively voting against the very Medicare drug price negotiation that 80% of Americans support.

Then there is the stock trading situation, which is less a footnote than a flashing neon sign. In early 2026, it was revealed that Letlow failed to disclose over 210 individual stock trades as required by the STOCK Act, with more than 100 of those reported over a year late [5]. The trades, valued between $225,000 and $3.3 million, included shares of Magnificent Seven tech giants like Apple, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta, plus healthcare stocks and a Micron Technology purchase that rose over 370% [6]. Letlow blamed her financial adviser at Merrill Lynch, saying the firm made trades without consulting her and was unaware of the disclosure requirements. She now says she supports a bill to ban insider stock trading by members of Congress [5] -- a conversion that arrived, conveniently, at the exact moment she launched her 2026 Senate campaign and the headlines caught fire.

The bottom line on Julia Letlow is that she has done real work on paid family leave and Social Security -- two issues where she breaks from the typical Republican mold -- but her 210 undisclosed stock trades undercut any claim to transparency or reform. She is running for Senate as a candidate of the people while sitting on a portfolio of late-filed trades worth millions. On the twenty bills 80% of Americans support, she delivers on roughly two and hopes the tragic backstory and the Senate ambition will cover the gap.
